name: Energy Sector (Global)
summary: Day 50 evening. XLE $56.58 (+1.5% Thu), Exxon $151.98 (+2.0% Thu), Cheniere $262.74 (+2.3% Thu — structural US LNG gap-fill). US markets CLOSED Good Friday; Thursday closes roll unchanged into Monday. Brent SETTLED Friday $96.16 (-3.3% net, recovered from $92.92 morning low); WTI diverged to $87.77 (-7.3%, Brent-WTI spread $8.39 widest since blockade). Heating Oil -8.7% Friday = largest single-asset move today, distillate-supply-crisis trade fully unwound. RBOB -4.2%. CF Industries +3.2% Thu + Cheniere +2.3% Thu = physical-disruption trade holding firm while war-premium plays (ITA -2.1%, Frontline -1.0%) sold off. Three-layer mispricing intact: physical Dated Brent ~$132 vs futures $96 vs equity-implied $85-90. If Round 2 fails, refined products snap back +6%+ session. EIA -9.13M bbl crude draw IGNORED by deal-sentiment market.

signals.content: XLE +1.7% on the day while S&P -1.7% and Nifty -2.1%. The divergence is widening — energy is the only sector gaining during the crisis. Exxon +3.4% ($170.99), Cheniere +1.9% ($296.91, +54.6% quarterly). Defense sector (ITA) diverging — now -11% monthly despite war, due to Pentagon budget uncertainty. The rotation is oil/gas, not defense. (Sources: yfinance)

Energy sector collapsed on de-escalation signals — XLE $58.97 (-3.7% 1D) while S&P 500 +0.7% = explicit market signal that oil falling is good for the economy but bad for energy producers. Exxon $160.78 (-5.2% 1D) — the most direct oil proxy took the biggest hit. Cheniere $275.84 (-2.8% 1D) — LNG proxy, because if Hormuz reopens, Qatar's LNG scarcity premium evaporates. CF Industries -1.4% (fertilizer proxy). These are 90%+ crisis-linked moves. If de-escalation is confirmed, XLE could return from $59 toward the $45-50 pre-war range. However, if the April 6 deadline expires without extension, this entire move reverses. Monthly still +5.0% and quarterly +30.0% — the crisis premium is not unwound, just shaved. CONFIRMED. (Sources: yfinance)

XLE Energy Sector at $58.97. WTI +11.9% (largest single-session move) should have LIFTED energy stocks but XLE showing -3.7% from April 1 close — the lag reflects April 2 US session pre-address data. Exxon at $160.78 (-5.2% on April 1 de-escalation). The energy sector is whipsawing on peace/war signals — massively long-term beneficiary (+38.6% 3M) but short-term hostage to headline sentiment. (Sources: yfinance)

XLE (energy ETF) at $59.25 (+0.5% 1D, +6.1% monthly, +27.2% 3M). Exxon at $160.69 (+7.3% monthly). But XLE is underperforming crude significantly: +6.1% monthly vs Brent +28.5% monthly. Energy stocks are pricing 'peak oil' / demand destruction expectations, NOT tracking crude higher. The sector is a war beneficiary but trading below the crude-implied level — this divergence suggests the market believes either (1) oil prices will not persist at $109, or (2) demand destruction will cap earnings even if prices stay high. Cheniere +12.9% monthly ($281.16) — LNG plays outperforming broader energy. CF Industries +24.1% monthly ($129.97) — fertilizer crisis play outperforming. OPEC+ pre-committed to pump post-ceasefire, which puts a ceiling on the post-war energy sector — if peace arrives, XLE could revert toward the $45-50 pre-war range.

DEFENSE/ENERGY DIVERGENCE FLIPPING — XLE +0.7% to $59.68 on April 6, +6.4% 1M, +33.1% 3M; Exxon $163.37 new high (+1.7% 1D, +8.4% 1M, +38.8% 3M). Cheniere LNG $284.05 (+1.0% 1D, +13.8% 1M, +45.3% 3M). Energy sector grinding higher with crude (WTI $116.48, Brent $111.75). The earlier laggard pattern (XLE underperforming crude) is now narrowing as the war becomes structural rather than transient. Q1 earnings season (begins April 10) will print blowout numbers for energy companies.

South Pars #2 strike eliminates ~85% of Iranian petrochemical output and re-prices Qatar exposure. South Pars/North Dome shared geological structure means Qatar's $200B+ LNG expansion is now exposed to escalation precedent. While Israel struck the Iranian petrochemical plant (NOT the gas reservoir), the precedent is alarming: reservoir-shared infrastructure is now under fire. Cheniere and US LNG names are the structural beneficiaries — captures the LNG export premium that Henry Hub does not.

ENERGY SECTOR ROTATION REVERSAL INCOMING ON CEASEFIRE [REPORTED]. Brent crashed -13.5% to $94.47 on the ceasefire announcement (biggest single-day fall since 1991 Gulf War), and the energy sector trade unwinds in lockstep on Wednesday US open. XLE closed Tuesday at $60.16 (+0.80% on the day) — the relief reversal will print sharply negative. Exxon Mobil closed Tuesday at $163.37 (+1.7% Tue) and is the largest XLE component most exposed. Cheniere LNG ($284.05 Tue close, +1.0%) faces a separate but related compression — TTF gas reversal compresses the US LNG export premium spread. Frontline (FRO) at $36.48 (-0.3% Tue) faces VLCC rate normalization as Hormuz traffic resumes — the spot AG-Far East rate has already fallen from $445K mid-March peak to ~$112K TCE because traffic stopped, not because risk reduced. CRITICAL DISTINCTION: the energy sector unwind today is the SAME phenomenon as the gold-up regime shift — both are dollar-weakness expressions. As DXY -1.1% to 98.92, oil-denominated equity prices compress on the dollar leg even as commodity prices fall on the supply leg. Long-term thesis (Hormuz selective control persists, war risk insurance restoration takes weeks per Lloyd's, fair value Brent $98-105 not $94) suggests today's XLE weakness is overdone — fair value XLE is closer to $58 than $54 if Brent recovers to $98-100.

Energy sector XLE -3.5% to $58.05 on April 8 ceasefire as oil crashed -11.5%. Exxon -4.7% to $156.22. Cheniere (LNG) -3.2% to $275.21 — LNG war premium unwinding. Energy was the biggest loser sector on the ceasefire day. BUT: energy still +25.2% 3M (XLE), +26.2% (Exxon) — war gains far from fully unwound. If ceasefire collapses and oil snaps back to $110+, energy leads the reversal. CF Industries -5.7% was the most extreme sector reversal — fertilizer/nitrogen more exposed to Qatar disruption normalization hopes. (Source: yfinance, CNBC)

Day 45, Ceasefire Day 5. XLE $56.94 (-0.7% Friday), Exxon $152.51 (-1.6%), Cheniere $265.54 (-0.1%). Energy sector pulling back on ceasefire optimism — war premium deflating. Islamabad talks failed Saturday; Monday repricing likely reverses the pullback. If Brent gaps above $100, energy sector rallies. (Source: yfinance)

Energy complex selloff accelerating on de-escalation trade: XLE -2.0% to $55.95, Exxon -2.2% to $149.24, Heating Oil -3.6%, Gasoline -2.5%. Refined products leading the selloff — carry LARGER war premiums than crude because refinery margins expanded during crisis. Energy equities now pricing $85-90 Brent while actual Brent at $95.21 and physical Dated Brent at $120+. THREE layers of disconnection. CF Industries -2.2% (fertilizer war premium unwinding). Frontline -2.0% (tanker de-escalation trade). Sector rotation explicitly visible: XLE -2.0% while S&P +1.2%. Q1 earnings rotation: Goldman beat pulling flows to financials FROM energy. PPI relief (+0.5% vs 1.1%) reduces inflation premium in energy producers. If 'deal within days' narrative breaks, entire trade reverses violently. (Sources: yfinance, market data)

EVENING UPDATE: De-escalation rotation confirmed at close. XLE $55.95 (-2.0% 1D, -2.4% 1M, +18.3% 3M), Exxon $149.24 (-2.2%). Equity-implied Brent at $85-90 based on XLE pricing — THREE LAYERS below physical reality: Physical Dated Brent $120-144 > Brent futures $95.78 > Equity-implied ~$85-90. The energy sector is pricing a deal that hasn't happened. S&P hit new post-war high 6,967 while XLE sold off — explicit sector rotation from energy into tech/financials. Goldman Q1 beat pulling flows. If 'deal within days' narrative collapses by Thursday, XLE reverses +3-5% in a single session. Refined products leading: Heating Oil -2.2%, Gasoline -2.2%. (Sources: yfinance, market data)

price_snapshots.note: XLE $58.97 (-3.7% 1D), Exxon $160.78 (-5.2% 1D), Cheniere $275.84 (-2.8% 1D) — de-escalation selloff exceeds 3% threshold

edges.context: XLE -3.5% on ceasefire as Brent crashed -11.5%. Energy biggest loser sector. But still +25.2% 3M — war gains far from fully unwound. If ceasefire collapses, energy leads reversal.
XLE -3.5% while S&P +2.51% on ceasefire = capital rotating from energy to broad market. Airlines +11-14%, tech surged. Energy sector weight declining.

trigger_points.condition: Energy sector becomes largest S&P 500 sector by weight
trigger_points.mechanism: Would signal a structural regime shift in the US equity market — from tech-led to energy-led. Last happened briefly in 2008 and 2022.
